When selection is weak at all loci (the infinitesimal model), the arguments leading to Equation 26.15a yield the expected response starting with a founder population of size $ N_0 $ as $$ R_{N_{0}}(t)=R(t)\left(1-\frac{1}{2N_{0}}\right) $$ (26.24a) where $ R(t) $ is the response expected when the initial base population is infinite (Equation 26.15a). More generally, if two replicate populations of the same size are created using different numbe

formulas.label: Formula 26.24b
formulas.section: EFFECTS OF POPULATION STRUCTURE ON LONG-TERM RESPONSE
formulas.subsection: Founder Effects and Population Bottlenecks
formulas.context_text: e populations of the same size are created using different numbers of founders ( $ N_{01}, N_{02} $) from a common, and large, base population, the ratio of the expected response at any generation is $$ \frac{R_{N_{1}}}{R_{N_{2}}}=\frac{1-1/(2N_{01})}{1-1/(2N_{02})} $$ (26.24b)

formulas.label: Formula 26.25a
formulas.section: EFFECTS OF POPULATION STRUCTURE ON LONG-TERM RESPONSE
formulas.subsection: Founder Effects and Population Bottlenecks
formulas.context_text: n selection on a locus is strong ( $ 2N_{e}s \gg 1 $), the probability that a selected line formed from a bottlenecked base population will eventually become fixed for the favored allele converges to $$ u_{N_{0}}(p_{0})=1-(1-p_{0})^{2N_{0}} $$ (26.25a) where $ p_0 $ is the major-allele frequency in the population being sampled. This follows because if selection is sufficiently strong, the favored allele will become fixed if it is found in the initi

formulas.label: Formula 26.25b
formulas.section: EFFECTS OF POPULATION STRUCTURE ON LONG-TERM RESPONSE
formulas.subsection: Founder Effects and Population Bottlenecks
formulas.context_text: ility of $ 1 - (1 - p_0)^{2N_0} $. Using this approximation, the ratio of the expected limiting contribution from such a locus to the expected contribution when the founding population is infinite is $$ \frac{u_{N_{0}}(p_{0})-p_{0}}{u(p_{0})-p_{0}}\simeq\frac{1-(1-p_{0})^{2N_{0}}-p_{0}}{1-p_{0}}=1-(1-p_{0})^{2N_{0}-1}\simeq1-e^{-p_{o}(2N_{0}-1)} $$ (26.25b) A more accurate measure would be to weight the fixation probability, $ u(p) $, by the sampling probability given a starting allele frequency, $ \sum_{i=1}^{2N_0} \Pr(i \mid p_0, 2N_0) u(i / [2N_0]) $

formulas.subsection: Population Subdivision
formulas.context_text: (compare Equations 7.19b and 7.20c). Recall from Equation 7.20c that the measure, f, of departures from Hardy-Weinberg frequencies enters into the selection coefficients. Caballero et al. showed that $$ f=\frac{N_{FS}-1}{4N_{TM}-3N_{FS}+3}+f_{r} $$ (26.26a) where $ N_{FS} $ is the number of full-sib matings, $ N_{TM} $ is the total number of matings, and $ f_{r} $ is the departure from Hardy-Weinberg genotype frequencies under random mating in a finite

formulas.label: Formula 26.26b

formulas.subsection: Population Subdivision
formulas.context_text: r of full-sib matings, $ N_{TM} $ is the total number of matings, and $ f_{r} $ is the departure from Hardy-Weinberg genotype frequencies under random mating in a finite population, which is given by $$ f_{r}=-\left(\frac{1}{8N_{f}}+\frac{1}{8N_{m}}\right) $$ (26.26b) where $ N_{m} $ and $ N_{f} $ are the numbers of reproducing males and females. Note that the negative sign implies that under random mating, there is a slight expected excessive of heterozygotes rel

formulas.label: Formula 26.27
formulas.section: ASYMPTOTIC RESPONSE DUE TO MUTATIONAL INPUT
formulas.subsection: Results for the Infinitesimal Model
formulas.context_text: We start by assuming complete additivity and ignore any effects of gametic-phase disequilibrium. From Equation 11.20b, the expected additive genetic variance at generation t is $$ \sigma_{A}^{2}(t)\simeq2N_{e}\sigma_{m}^{2}+\left[\sigma_{A}^{2}(0)-2N_{e}\sigma_{m}^{2}\right]\exp(-t/2N_{e}) $$ (26.27)

formulas.label: Formula 26.28a
formulas.section: ASYMPTOTIC RESPONSE DUE TO MUTATIONAL INPUT
formulas.subsection: Results for the Infinitesimal Model
formulas.context_text: Setting $ \sigma_{A}^{2}(0)=0 $ gives the additive variance contributed entirely from mutation as $$ \sigma_{A,m}^{2}(t)\simeq2N_{e}\sigma_{m}^{2}\left[1-\exp(-t/2N_{e})\right] $$ (26.28a)

formulas.label: Formula 26.28b
formulas.section: ASYMPTOTIC RESPONSE DUE TO MUTATIONAL INPUT
formulas.subsection: Results for the Infinitesimal Model
formulas.context_text: Hence, the rate of response at generation t from mutational input is $$ r_{m}(t)=\overline{\imath}\frac{\sigma_{A,m}^{2}(t)}{\sigma_{z}}\simeq2N_{e}\overline{\imath}\frac{\sigma_{m}^{2}}{\sigma_{z}}\left[1-\exp(-t/2N_{e})\right] $$ (26.28b) where we have made the usual assumption that the phenotypic variance, $ \sigma_z^2 $, does not significantly change over time and that any disequilibrium is ignored. For $ t \gg 2N_e $, the per-gener

formulas.label: Formula 26.29
formulas.section: ASYMPTOTIC RESPONSE DUE TO MUTATIONAL INPUT
formulas.subsection: Results for the Infinitesimal Model
formulas.context_text: phenotypic variance, $ \sigma_z^2 $, does not significantly change over time and that any disequilibrium is ignored. For $ t \gg 2N_e $, the per-generation response approaches an asymptotic limit of $$ r_{m}(\infty)=2N_{e}\bar{\imath}\frac{\sigma_{m}^{2}}{\sigma_{z}} $$ (26.29)

formulas.label: Formula 26.30a
formulas.section: ASYMPTOTIC RESPONSE DUE TO MUTATIONAL INPUT
formulas.subsection: Results for the Infinitesimal Model
formulas.context_text: Summing Equation 26.28b over generations (using the approximation given by Equation 7.28b) yields a cumulative response due to new mutation of $$ R_{m}(t)=\sum_{\tau=1}^{t}r_{m}(\tau)\simeq2N_{e}\overline{\imath}\frac{\sigma_{m}^{2}}{\sigma_{z}}\bigg(t-2N_{e}[1-\exp(-t/2N_{e})]\bigg) $$ (26.30a) as found by Hill (1982a, 1990) and Weber and Diggins (1990). An approximation for genes of sufficiently large effect ( $ |a| \gg \sigma_z/N\bar{i} $) is to consider them as being essentially fixed in

formulas.label: Formula 26.30b

Note by comparison with Equation 26.29 that the instantaneous fixation assumption is equivalent to assuming that the asymptotic rate of response applies from generation
